message.content:
Below is a **testing checklist** built only from **Google Search Central** (and closely related Google sources like RFC references mentioned there). For each check I give:

- **Check name (testable condition)**
- **What it verifies / why it matters**
- **Google Search Central topic title**
- **Exact Google URL**

I’ll group checks by area so you can turn each bullet into a discrete automated test or QA scenario.

---

## 1. rel=canonical link presence, placement, syntax

### 1.1 Canonical link present on duplicate/variant pages

- **Check**  
  For pages that are *duplicates or very similar variants* of another page, verify a **`<link rel="canonical" href="...">`** exists in the HTML (or an equivalent HTTP header) pointing to the canonical URL.

- **Verifies**  
  That the site explicitly indicates a preferred URL for duplicate or very similar content using a **strong canonicalization signal** instead of leaving it entirely to Google’s choice.[1]  
  Google lists **`rel="canonical"` link annotations** as one of the *strong signals* used to pick a canonical URL.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → How to specify a canonical URL for your site**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 1.2 Canonical link is in the `<head>`, not `<body>`

- **Check**  
  Confirm that the **`<link rel="canonical" ...>` element appears in the `<head>` section**, not outside it.

- **Verifies**  
  That the canonical link meets Google’s acceptance requirements: Google only accepts the canonical **if it appears in the `<head>`** and requires at least the `<head>` to be valid HTML.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → The `rel="canonical"` link element**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 1.3 Canonical link uses an absolute URL

- **Check**  
  Validate that the **`href` of the canonical tag is an absolute URL** (scheme + host, not a relative path).

- **Verifies**  
  Conforms to Google’s guidance that **absolute paths** should be used with both HTML `rel=canonical` and the canonical HTTP header.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → Use absolute paths**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 1.4 Canonical tag present for non‑HTML documents via HTTP header

- **Check**  
  For non‑HTML resources (PDF, Word, etc.), confirm that the HTTP response sends a **`Link: <...>; rel="canonical"` header** when a canonical should be declared.

- **Verifies**  
  That non‑HTML content uses the **canonical HTTP header** method, which Google explicitly supports for documents including PDF and other non‑HTML formats.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → `rel="canonical"` HTTP header**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

## 2. Self‑referencing canonical

### 2.1 Canonical points to the page’s own preferred URL

- **Check**  
  On pages that are **already the canonical/preferred version**, verify that the canonical tag (or header) **self‑references** the exact canonical URL (including scheme, host, and path normalization you want chosen).

- **Verifies**  
  That the canonical tag is used to **confirm the page as its own canonical** and to signal the preferred URL among multiple technically valid variants.[1]  
  Google recommends **picking a canonical URL for each page** and specifying it via canonicalization signals (including `rel=canonical`).[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → Pick a canonical URL for your duplicate pages**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

*(Google does not state “self‑canonical is mandatory,” but this is an accepted best practice derived from “pick a canonical URL for each of your pages and submit them in a sitemap” and the role of `rel=canonical` as a strong signal.)[1]*

---

## 3. Conflicting or multiple canonicals

### 3.1 Multiple HTML canonicals on the same page

- **Check**  
  Ensure there is **at most one `<link rel="canonical">`** element in the `<head>` of each page.

- **Verifies**  
  Avoids **ambiguous or conflicting canonical signals** from multiple canonical tags, which can cause Google to ignore them and rely on other signals.  
  While the page doesn’t explicitly say “multiple canonicals are invalid,” it emphasizes that canonical annotations are a **strong signal** and should clearly point to one URL; conflicting signals undermine that.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → `rel="canonical"` link element**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 3.2 Conflicting HTML canonical vs HTTP header canonical

- **Check**  
  If both a **HTML `rel=canonical` tag** and a **canonical HTTP header** are present, confirm that they **point to the same URL**.

- **Verifies**  
  That you are not sending **conflicting canonical instructions** via different methods.  
  Google allows two ways to provide canonical annotations (HTML and HTTP header) and describes them as functionally equivalent signals; they should be consistent for a single resource.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → You can provide the `rel="canonical"` link annotations in two ways**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 3.3 Canonical conflicts with redirects

- **Check**  
  If a URL **redirects** (e.g., 301) to another URL, check that any canonical set on the **final destination** does **not conflict** with the redirect chain (for example, redirect A → B but canonical on B points to C or back to A).

- **Verifies**  
  That **redirects (a strong signal)** and canonical tags **reinforce the same canonical choice** instead of sending mixed signals. Google lists **redirects** as a strong canonicalization signal and recommends using them to deprecate duplicates.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → How to specify a canonical URL for your site → Redirects**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

## 4. Canonical in HTTP header

### 4.1 `Link: rel="canonical"` header syntax and absolute URL

- **Check**  
  For responses using an HTTP canonical, verify that the **`Link` header is syntactically correct** and uses an **absolute URL** for the canonical.

- **Verifies**  
  Compliance with Google’s description of the **canonical HTTP header** mechanism and its requirement to use **absolute URLs**, as defined per RFC 5988 and Search Central guidance.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → `rel="canonical"` HTTP header**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 4.2 Header canonical used for non‑HTML and HTML consistently

- **Check**  
  When a site uses header‑based canonicalization (especially for non‑HTML), ensure **no contradictory HTML `<link rel=canonical>`** is also present that points elsewhere.

- **Verifies**  
  That you have a **single consistent canonical signal** per resource when using the HTTP header method; Search Central positions HTML and HTTP header canonical annotations as **alternate implementations of the same signal**.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → You can provide the `rel="canonical"` link annotations in two ways**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

## 5. Canonical vs XML sitemap consistency

### 5.1 Canonical URLs appear in sitemap(s)

- **Check**  
  Confirm that **only canonical URLs** (or primarily canonical URLs) are listed in your XML sitemaps, and that the **canonical tag on each URL listed in the sitemap either self‑references or matches the sitemap URL**.

- **Verifies**  
  That sitemap URLs are aligned with canonical choices. Google treats **sitemap inclusion as a weak canonical signal** and recommends **picking a canonical URL for each page and submitting them in a sitemap**.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → Sitemap**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 5.2 No sitemap URLs that are non‑canonical duplicates

- **Check**  
  Ensure that **duplicate or parameterized variants** that are not meant to be canonical are either **omitted from sitemaps** or have sitemaps that clearly favor the canonical URLs.

- **Verifies**  
  That sitemaps are not **contradicting canonicalization by promoting duplicates** as primary URLs. Search Central notes that all pages in a sitemap are **suggested as canonicals**, so including duplicates weakens the signal.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → All pages listed in a sitemap are suggested as canonicals**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

## 6. Duplicate URLs via parameters

### 6.1 Parameter variants point to a single canonical URL

- **Check**  
  For URLs that differ only by **query parameters** (sorting, tracking, faceting, etc.), verify that each variant:

  - Sends a **canonical tag or header** that points to the canonical URL, **or**
  - Redirects to the canonical URL when appropriate.

- **Verifies**  
  That parameter‑driven duplicates are consolidated to a single canonical URL using **strong signals** (redirects or `rel=canonical`). Search Central’s main canonicalization guidance uses an example of many URLs accessing the same content and instructs to point them at one canonical URL.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → Example of many URLs for the same content**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 6.2 Consistent canonicalization for faceted or sorted pages

- **Check**  
  For faceted navigation or sort orders that create largely similar content, verify a **clear canonical strategy**: either each variant has a canonical to the main page or only specific variants are self‑canonical and the rest are noindex/blocked per site strategy.

- **Verifies**  
  That near‑duplicate parameterized pages are handled in line with Google’s concept of **duplicate or very similar pages** and that canonical signals are used to consolidate them.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → Duplicate or very similar pages**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

*(Parameter‑handling details are often managed via internal rules now that the old URL Parameters tool has been deprecated; the core canonical guidance remains in this document.)[1]*

---

## 7. www / non‑www consolidation

### 7.1 One host (www vs non‑www) selected as canonical

- **Check**  
  For each page, verify that **only one host variant** (e.g. `https://www.example.com/...` or `https://example.com/...`) is treated as canonical by:

  - Redirecting the non‑canonical host to the canonical host, **or**
  - Using a canonical tag from the non‑canonical host to the canonical host.

- **Verifies**  
  That **slightly different URLs for the same page** across hostnames are consolidated. Google defines canonicalization as choosing one URL out of many possible variants (e.g., different hostnames) and lists **redirects and canonical tags** as strong signals.[1][6]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → How to specify a canonical URL for your site**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

*(The concept of canonicalization across www vs non‑www is explicitly covered in the “multiple URLs for the same content” context.)[1]*

---

## 8. HTTP / HTTPS consolidation

### 8.1 HTTPS preferred and signaled as canonical

- **Check**  
  When both HTTP and HTTPS variants exist, verify that:

  - HTTP URLs **redirect** to the HTTPS version, and/or
  - HTTP URLs send a **canonical tag pointing to the HTTPS URL**.

- **Verifies**  
  That HTTPS is explicitly favored as canonical. Google states that its systems prefer HTTPS by default and recommends reinforcing this by redirects, `rel="canonical"` from HTTP to HTTPS, or HSTS.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → HTTPS preference**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

### 8.2 No conflicting canonical from HTTPS back to HTTP

- **Check**  
  Ensure that **HTTPS pages never canonicalize back to HTTP** or redirect back to HTTP.

- **Verifies**  
  That canonical signals are aligned with Google’s documented **default preference for HTTPS**, and avoids confusing or circular canonicalization.[1]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → Although our systems prefer HTTPS pages over HTTP pages by default…**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

---

## 9. Trailing slash consistency

### 9.1 Single canonical for trailing‑slash vs non‑trailing‑slash URLs

- **Check**  
  For each path that can be reached as both `/path/` and `/path`, verify that:

  - Only one of them is indexed as canonical (via redirect or canonical tag), and
  - The canonical tag (if present) always uses the preferred version consistently.

- **Verifies**  
  That **URL variants differing only by trailing slash** are consolidated, in line with Google’s general definition of canonicalization as picking a preferred URL among multiple syntactic variants that serve the same content.[1][6]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Specify a canonical URL” → How to specify a canonical URL for your site**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls`[1]

*(Trailing slash vs non‑trailing slash is one form of “slightly different URLs for the same page” covered by Google’s canonicalization guidance.)[1]*

---

## 10. Canonicalization diagnostics and conflict detection (Search Console)

These are not page‑level tags themselves but are **checks you can build around Google’s own reporting**.

### 10.1 Google‑selected canonical vs user‑declared canonical

- **Check**  
  Using the URL Inspection API or manual inspection, compare:

  - **User‑declared canonical** (from your tags/headers/sitemaps), and  
  - **Google‑selected canonical**.

  Flag cases where Google chooses a different canonical for URLs that you intended to be canonical.

- **Verifies**  
  Whether your canonical signals are strong and consistent enough. Search Central explicitly instructs using URL Inspection to **check which page Google considers canonical**, noting that even if you designate a canonical, Google might pick a different one based on other signals.[2]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Fix canonicalization issues” → Use the URL Inspection tool**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/canonicalization-troubleshooting`[2]

---

### 10.2 Detect “Duplicate, Google chose a different canonical than user”

- **Check**  
  In Search Console reports, identify URLs categorized as **duplicates where Google chose a different canonical than the user** and correlate them with your on‑page canonical tags and sitemap entries.

- **Verifies**  
  That canonical signals are not being overridden due to **inconsistencies, weak signals, or incorrect configurations** such as CMS issues or server misconfigurations, which Google lists as common canonicalization problems.[2]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Fix canonicalization issues”**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/canonicalization-troubleshooting`[2]

---

## 11. Interaction with other signals: hreflang, language variants, and duplication

While not part of the tag syntax itself, these are canonicalization‑related checks Search Central highlights.

### 11.1 Canonical and hreflang consistent across language/region variants

- **Check**  
  For language or regional variants (`hreflang` clusters), verify that:

  - Each URL **declares the correct canonical** for its own content, and  
  - Canonical and `hreflang` annotations are not in conflict (for example, canonicalizing all locales to one region URL but declaring complex bidirectional `hreflang` across them).

- **Verifies**  
  That **language variants aren’t incorrectly canonicalized**, which can cause wrong pages to be chosen or indexed. Google lists **incorrect language annotations** as a common cause of canonicalization issues and recommends fixing them.[2]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Fix canonicalization issues” → Common issues with canonicalization**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/canonicalization-troubleshooting`[2]

---

### 11.2 Syndicated or scraped copies not canonicalized to your site

- **Check**  
  For known syndicated copies of your content, confirm that external partners either:

  - Use canonical tags pointing to **your original URL**, or  
  - Block indexing of the copies (as appropriate).

- **Verifies**  
  That **canonical authority remains with your original content**; Google flags **syndicated and copycat content** as canonicalization challenges and recommends partners block indexing or otherwise avoid competing for canonical status.[2]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  **“Fix canonicalization issues” → Syndicated content and copycat websites**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/canonicalization-troubleshooting`[2]

---

If you want, I can convert this into a machine‑readable checklist (e.g., JSON with `check_id`, `description`, `reference_url`) suitable for automated QA or Lighthouse‑style audits.
